title: Right upper quadrant pain after a fatty meal in a diabetic on digoxin

chart.oneLiner: 62F with T2DM, HFrEF, and rate-controlled atrial fibrillation on digoxin presents with ~14 hours of constant right-upper-quadrant and epigastric pain that began after a fatty meal, with nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, and subjective fever; she also reports two days of poor oral intake and feeling 'off'.

opening: I've had this constant pain under my right ribs and up here in the middle since last night — it started after dinner and it just won't quit. I feel sweaty and sick to my stomach.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 62-year-old woman, uncomfortable, diaphoretic, holding her right upper belly. Cooperative but tired and a little nauseated. She is a fair historian.

HPI: Constant RUQ AND epigastric pain for ~14h that began an hour after a fatty fried dinner, boring through to the right scapula/shoulder. Several episodes of nonbloody vomiting, sweaty, subjective fevers/chills. She has had several briefer episodes of similar post-meal RUQ pain over the past few months that resolved on their own within an hour or two (biliary colic) — reveal only if asked about prior episodes. For the past 2 days she has had a poor appetite and has barely eaten or drunk because of nausea, and feels generally 'off' and weak. NO black or pale stools, NO dark urine, NO yellowing of the eyes/skin that she has noticed (reveal only if asked about stool/urine color or jaundice). The pain does NOT change with exertion or breathing and there is NO pressure/tightness radiating to the left arm or jaw (reveal only if asked about chest pain / exertional component), though she does feel her heart is 'slow and skips'.
CARDIAC/ENDOCRINE BACKGROUND (reveal each only when the matching question is asked): She has atrial fibrillation and a 'weak heart pump' (heart failure) and was told her heart rate runs controlled on her pills. Over the last day or two — since she stopped eating and drinking well — she has felt more lightheaded, with some nausea and her vision looking 'a bit yellow/green and blurry' at times, and she thinks her pulse has felt slower than usual (these are subtle digoxin-toxicity clues; reveal only if asked about visual changes, lightheadedness, or how her medicines have been going). She has type 2 diabetes and takes several pills plus a 'newer sugar pill'.
PMH: type 2 diabetes mellitus; heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF ~35%); permanent atrial fibrillation; hypertension; obesity; chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine ~1.4). MEDS (reveal the list only if asked what she takes): metformin; empagliflozin (an SGLT2 inhibitor — the 'newer sugar pill'); digoxin 0.125 mg daily; diltiazem; lisinopril; hydrochlorothiazide; atorvastatin; apixaban. ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: retired teacher, 3 children, rare alcohol, never smoked. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: as above — poor intake x2 days, nausea, sweats; no diarrhea; no dysuria/flank pain; no overt jaundice noticed.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: uncomfortable, diaphoretic, mildly ill-appearing.
- Vitals: BP 108/64, HR 54 and irregular, RR 20, 38.1°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Abdomen: RUQ and epigastric tenderness with guarding; POSITIVE Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation); no rebound elsewhere; no distension; no palpable pulsatile mass.
- No scleral icterus appreciated. Bowel sounds present. Mucous membranes dry; skin tenting (volume depleted).
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular, bradycardic; no new murmur. Lungs: a few bibasilar crackles. No peripheral edema today.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the prior post-meal episodes, the digoxin/visual/lightheadedness clues, the poor oral intake's medication implications, or the absence of jaundice/dark urine unless the matching question is asked.
- Do NOT recite the medication list unless asked what medicines she takes.
- Murphy's sign and the volume-depletion/cardiac findings are revealed only if the relevant exam is performed.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 14.8 with left shift (neutrophil predominance, bands present), Hgb 12.9, Plt 268.
labs.LFTs: AST 56, ALT 60, Alk Phos 138 (mildly elevated), total bilirubin 1.3, direct bilirubin 0.4 — mild hepatocellular pattern, NOT an obstructive/cholestatic picture.
labs.Lipase: Normal at 42 (rules out acute pancreatitis as the primary process).
labs.BMP: Na 134, K 5.6, Cl 99, HCO3 17, BUN 42, Cr 2.1 (up from baseline 1.4 — acute-on-chronic kidney injury), glucose 168. Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 134 − (99 + 17) = 18 (elevated). Beta-hydroxybutyrate POSITIVE / ketones present despite near-normal glucose — SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic ketoacidosis on a background of poor intake and AKI.
labs.Mg_Trop: Magnesium 1.6 (low-normal), ionized calcium normal. High-sensitivity troponin 38 ng/L then 41 ng/L (mildly elevated, essentially flat — type-2/demand pattern, no significant rise-and-fall to indicate acute MI).
labs.Digoxin: Serum digoxin level 2.8 ng/mL (therapeutic 0.5–0.9 for heart failure; this is supratherapeutic). Consistent with digoxin toxicity precipitated by AKI — reduced renal clearance — plus volume depletion; the K of 5.6 also fits acute digoxin toxicity.
labs.RUQ_US: RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis with a stone in the gallbladder neck, gallbladder wall thickening (>4 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign. Common bile duct is NOT dilated (4 mm) and no choledocholithiasis is seen — argues against choledocholithiasis/cholangitis.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with a slow, regularized ventricular response (~54 bpm) and down-sloping 'scooped'/sagging ST-segment depression (Salvador Dalí sign) with the digoxin effect; no acute ST-elevation MI pattern. In context (supratherapeutic digoxin level, AKI, hyperkalemia, GI symptoms, visual changes) this is concerning for DIGOXIN TOXICITY rather than benign digoxin effect.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with slow/regularized ventricular response and sagging 'scooped' ST depression (digoxin effect/toxicity); no STEMI. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead EKG showing atrial fibrillation with a slow regularized response and scooped ST depression of digoxin effect/toxicity
searchTerms: digoxin toxicity ECG scooped ST depression
atrial fibrillation regularized junctional digoxin effect ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: An older patient on digoxin for atrial fibrillation becomes nauseated and lightheaded with blurred yellow-green vision; the rhythm strip is reviewed.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion / cephalization with small bilateral pleural effusions and a borderline-enlarged cardiac silhouette, consistent with her known HFrEF; lungs otherwise without focal consolidation; NO free air under the diaphragm. The mild congestion is a deliberate caution against aggressive IV fluid resuscitation.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: mild pulmonary vascular congestion/cephalization with small effusions and cardiomegaly (mild CHF); no subdiaphragmatic free air. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing mild pulmonary vascular congestion, small effusions, and cardiomegaly of chronic heart failure with no free air
searchTerms: mild pulmonary vascular congestion CHF chest x-ray
cardiomegaly cephalization small pleural effusions radiograph
cxr.clinicalVignette: A patient with known heart failure has a chest film before fluid resuscitation to gauge volume status.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). For this older diabetic woman on digoxin with RUQ/epigastric pain, name three must-not-miss diagnoses you have to exclude before committing.
steps.rubric: Acute cholecystitis (leading,5). Choledocholithiasis / ascending cholangitis — must-not-miss (CANT-MISS,4). Acute coronary syndrome / inferior MI presenting as epigastric pain with diaphoresis in a diabetic woman — must-not-miss (CANT-MISS,4). Digoxin toxicity as a concurrent must-not-miss given bradycardia, GI symptoms, and visual changes (CANT-MISS,3). Acute pancreatitis (core,2). Peptic ulcer disease (core,1). Named leading dx w/ justification — post-fatty-meal RUQ pain, prior biliary colic, Murphy's (core,3). Explicitly names three must-not-miss diagnoses (core,3). PENALTY: anchoring on a benign biliary cause and missing the cardiac/digoxin danger (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute cholecystitis (constant post-fatty-meal RUQ pain boring to the scapula, prior biliary colic, fever, positive Murphy's). Three must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: (1) Choledocholithiasis / ascending cholangitis (an obstructing CBD stone); (2) Acute coronary syndrome / inferior MI — a diabetic woman with epigastric pain and diaphoresis can have atypical ACS, so an EKG and troponin are mandatory; (3) Digoxin toxicity — she is bradycardic on digoxin with poor intake, nausea, lightheadedness, and visual changes, which is itself dangerous and must be checked. Also reasonable: acute pancreatitis and peptic ulcer disease.

coreActions.item: Named leading dx w/ justification — post-fatty-meal RUQ pain, prior biliary colic, Murphy's

penalties.item: Anchors on a benign biliary cause and misses the cardiac/digoxin danger

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: RUQ ultrasound as imaging of choice for biliary pain (CANT-MISS,4). EKG to exclude ACS / characterize the bradyarrhythmia (CANT-MISS,3). Serum digoxin level given digoxin use, AKI, and toxicity clues (CANT-MISS,2). BMP/electrolytes incl. renal function and anion gap (2). CBC (1). LFTs (1). Lipase (1). High-sensitivity troponin (bonus,1). CXR; magnesium; serum/urine ketones for the SGLT2 inhibitor (bonus,1). PENALTY: ordering CT with IV contrast in AKI without thought, or a shotgun panel with no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: RUQ ultrasound (imaging of choice for suspected cholecystitis); EKG (exclude ACS, characterize the bradycardia/AF); serum digoxin level; BMP with renal function and anion gap; CBC; LFTs; Lipase; high-sensitivity troponin (serial); magnesium; serum/urine ketones and venous gas given the SGLT2 inhibitor and gap acidosis; CXR. Avoid reflexive IV-contrast CT given her acute kidney injury.

coreActions.item: BMP / electrolytes with renal function and anion gap

bonusActions.item: CXR, magnesium, and ketones for the SGLT2 inhibitor

penalties.item: Reflexive IV-contrast CT in AKI or shotgun panel with no reasoning

optionalTests: CXR, magnesium, and ketones for the SGLT2 inhibitor
contraindicatedTests: Reflexive IV-contrast CT in AKI or shotgun panel with no reasoning

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state what they mean for her management.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with a SLOW, regularized ventricular response (~54 bpm) and down-sloping 'scooped'/sagging ST depression (digoxin effect). With the supratherapeutic digoxin level, AKI, hyperkalemia, GI symptoms, and visual changes this is DIGOXIN TOXICITY, not benign effect; and there is NO acute STEMI pattern to explain the pain. CREDIT: identifies AF with a slow/regularized ventricular response (5); recognizes the scooped ST depression of digoxin effect and, in context, names digoxin TOXICITY (5); states there is no acute STEMI, so ACS is not the primary cause of the abdominal pain (3). PENALTY: calling it a benign 'digoxin effect' with no concern for toxicity, mislabeling it a primary STEMI, or missing the bradyarrhythmia entirely (-3).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation with a slow, regularized ventricular response (~54 bpm)
scooped/sagging ST depression of digoxin effect; no STEMI
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with a slow regularized ventricular response and scooped ST depression — digoxin toxicity in context; no acute STEMI.
commonMistakes: calling it a benign 'digoxin effect' and missing toxicity in the clinical context
mislabeling the scooped ST depression as a primary STEMI
missing the bradyarrhythmia / regularization of AF
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation (no organized P waves, irregular baseline) with a slow and partly regularized ventricular response at ~54 bpm, plus down-sloping 'scooped'/sagging ST-segment depression — the digoxin effect. Given her supratherapeutic digoxin level, acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, nausea, and yellow-green visual changes, this pattern signifies DIGOXIN TOXICITY, not a benign effect. There is no acute ST-elevation MI pattern, so ACS is not the primary explanation for her abdominal pain — it lets me commit to the biliary diagnosis while treating the digoxin toxicity in parallel.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with a slow regularized ventricular response and scooped ST depression — digoxin toxicity in context; no acute STEMI.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation with a slow, regularized ventricular response (~54 bpm)
scooped/sagging ST depression of digoxin effect; no STEMI

penalties.label: calls it benign digoxin effect / mislabels a STEMI / misses the bradyarrhythmia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings and how they affect your plan.
steps.rubric: Findings = mild pulmonary vascular congestion/cephalization with small bilateral effusions and a borderline-enlarged heart (her known HFrEF); NO focal consolidation; NO free air under the diaphragm. CREDIT: identifies the mild congestion/effusions of heart failure (5); states the practical consequence — give fluids cautiously/titrated rather than large boluses, and notes there is no subdiaphragmatic free air to suggest perforation (5). PENALTY: calling the film entirely normal and missing the congestion (which would license unsafe aggressive fluids), or over-reading it as florid pulmonary edema/pneumonia (-3).
keyFindings: mild pulmonary vascular congestion with small effusions and cardiomegaly (CHF)
no free air under the diaphragm; resuscitate with cautious, titrated fluids
criticalFindings: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion/effusions of chronic heart failure — fluids must be cautious/titrated; no subdiaphragmatic free air.
commonMistakes: calling the film normal and licensing aggressive fluid boluses
over-reading mild congestion as florid pulmonary edema or pneumonia
steps.idealAnswer: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion/cephalization with small bilateral pleural effusions and a borderline-enlarged cardiac silhouette — consistent with her known HFrEF rather than an acute process; no focal consolidation and, importantly, no free air under the diaphragm to suggest a perforated viscus. Practically, she is volume-depleted from poor intake and vomiting but has a congestible heart, so I will resuscitate with cautious, titrated fluids and reassess rather than give large boluses.

criticalActions: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion/effusions of chronic heart failure — fluids must be cautious/titrated; no subdiaphragmatic free air.
coreActions: mild pulmonary vascular congestion with small effusions and cardiomegaly (CHF)
no free air under the diaphragm; resuscitate with cautious, titrated fluids

penalties.label: calls the film normal / over-reads florid edema or pneumonia

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the ultrasound, labs, EKG, and chest film, what is your diagnosis and reasoning? Commit to the most likely cause of her pain, name the discriminating findings, and list three mechanisms driving her acute kidney injury and elevated anion gap.
steps.rubric: Acute cholecystitis as the cause of the pain (6). Cites the discriminators — gallstones + wall thickening + pericholecystic fluid + sonographic Murphy with a NON-dilated CBD and near-normal bilirubin arguing against choledocholithiasis/cholangitis, and a flat (demand-pattern) troponin with no STEMI arguing against ACS. Names the CONCURRENT problems that are not the biliary disease — digoxin toxicity (level 2.8 with AKI and hyperkalemia) and SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic ketoacidosis. Names three mechanisms for the AKI/anion gap: pre-renal volume depletion from poor intake and vomiting; euglycemic ketoacidosis from empagliflozin during an acute illness; and an ACE-inhibitor/diuretic (lisinopril + HCTZ) contribution on chronic kidney disease (lactate/uremia also acceptable) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Most likely cause of the pain: Acute cholecystitis — the ultrasound shows cholelithiasis with a neck stone, gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign; the CBD is not dilated and bilirubin is near-normal, which argues against choledocholithiasis/ascending cholangitis, and the high-sensitivity troponin is only mildly elevated and flat (a demand/type-2 pattern) with no STEMI, which argues against ACS as the source. She has TWO concurrent, separate problems layered on top: (1) digoxin toxicity — a supratherapeutic level of 2.8 ng/mL precipitated by acute kidney injury and volume depletion, with bradycardia, hyperkalemia (K 5.6), GI symptoms, and yellow-green visual changes; and (2) SGLT2-inhibitor (empagliflozin)-associated euglycemic ketoacidosis — positive ketones with an anion gap of 18 despite a glucose of only 168. Three mechanisms for the AKI and elevated anion gap: pre-renal volume depletion from two days of poor intake plus vomiting; euglycemic ketoacidosis from empagliflozin during an acute illness; and the lisinopril + hydrochlorothiazide reducing renal perfusion/clearance on a background of stage-3 CKD. The discriminating tests were the RUQ ultrasound (biliary vs ductal), the serial troponin/EKG (biliary vs cardiac), and the digoxin level with the BMP/ketones (the toxic-metabolic overlay).

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? Address her digoxin toxicity and her medications, and state explicitly whether she can be safely discharged.
steps.rubric: Per MGH biliary disease + the toxic-metabolic overlay: NPO + CAUTIOUS/titrated IV fluids given HFrEF and pulmonary congestion (CANT-MISS,3); antibiotics — piperacillin-tazobactam OR ceftriaxone + metronidazole, renally dosed (CANT-MISS,4); manage digoxin toxicity — hold digoxin, treat hyperkalemia, give DigiFab if hemodynamically unstable/refractory, atropine/pacing for symptomatic bradycardia, AVOID calcium for hyperkalemia in dig toxicity, check magnesium (CANT-MISS,5); hold/adjust the offending meds — hold empagliflozin (euglycemic DKA), metformin, lisinopril and HCTZ in AKI (3); early laparoscopic cholecystectomy once stabilized, with surgery consult (4); analgesia + antiemetics (2); ADMIT — explicitly NOT safe for discharge: surgical source plus digoxin toxicity, AKI, and euglycemic ketoacidosis; arrange telemetry/monitoring, med reconciliation, and close follow-up (3). PENALTY: discharging her / no source-control plan (-4); giving IV calcium for the hyperkalemia in digoxin toxicity or large fluid boluses into a congested heart (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Admit — she is NOT safe for discharge: she has a surgical source (acute cholecystitis) plus digoxin toxicity, acute kidney injury, and euglycemic ketoacidosis, and needs telemetry. Biliary care: NPO; CAUTIOUS, titrated IV fluids (she is volume-depleted but has HFrEF with pulmonary congestion on CXR, so reassess frequently rather than bolus); renally dosed antibiotics — piperacillin-tazobactam OR ceftriaxone + metronidazole; analgesia and antiemetics; surgery consult for early laparoscopic cholecystectomy once she is medically stabilized (percutaneous cholecystostomy if she becomes too high-risk for surgery). Digoxin toxicity: hold digoxin; treat the hyperkalemia but AVOID IV calcium in digoxin toxicity; check and replete magnesium; atropine and transcutaneous pacing for symptomatic bradycardia; give digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab) if she is hemodynamically unstable, has a life-threatening arrhythmia, or refractory hyperkalemia. Medication reconciliation in AKI: hold empagliflozin (driving euglycemic ketoacidosis), hold metformin, and hold lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide; continue anticoagulation decision-making around surgery. Arrange admission with telemetry, nephrology/cardiology input as needed, and close follow-up.

criticalActions.item: NPO and cautious / titrated IV fluids given HFrEF and pulmonary congestion
Renally dosed antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
Manage digoxin toxicity — hold digoxin, treat hyperkalemia (no IV calcium), DigiFab if unstable, atropine/pacing, repleted magnesium

coreActions.item: Hold the offending meds — empagliflozin (euglycemic DKA), metformin, lisinopril and HCTZ in AKI
Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy once stabilized, with surgery consult
Admit — explicitly not safe for discharge; telemetry, med reconciliation, and close follow-up

bonusActions.item: Percutaneous cholecystostomy if too high-risk for surgery

penalties.item: IV calcium for hyperkalemia in digoxin toxicity or large fluid boluses into a congested heart

requiredActions: NPO and cautious / titrated IV fluids given HFrEF and pulmonary congestion
Renally dosed antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
Manage digoxin toxicity — hold digoxin, treat hyperkalemia (no IV calcium), DigiFab if unstable, atropine/pacing, repleted magnesium
optionalActions: Hold the offending meds — empagliflozin (euglycemic DKA), metformin, lisinopril and HCTZ in AKI
Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy once stabilized, with surgery consult
Admit — explicitly not safe for discharge; telemetry, med reconciliation, and close follow-up
Percutaneous cholecystostomy if too high-risk for surgery
unsafeActions: IV calcium for hyperkalemia in digoxin toxicity or large fluid boluses into a congested heart

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 62F with T2DM, HFrEF, rate-controlled atrial fibrillation on digoxin, and stage-3 CKD presents with ~14 hours of constant RUQ/epigastric pain after a fatty meal, with nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, and fever. The diagnosis is Acute cholecystitis, confirmed on ultrasound, but the picture is layered with two concurrent emergencies: digoxin toxicity (supratherapeutic level precipitated by AKI and poor intake) and SGLT2-inhibitor-associated euglycemic ketoacidosis. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (including medications and digoxin-toxicity clues), performing the targeted exam, interpreting an integrated EKG and CXR, and excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (choledocholithiasis/cholangitis and ACS).
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: RUQ and epigastric tenderness with guarding; POSITIVE Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on RUQ palpation); no rebound elsewhere; no distension; no palpable pulsatile mass.

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals — note the bradycardia and the digoxin/HFrEF/diabetes/CKD background.
Elicit the focused history, including the medication list and the digoxin-toxicity clues (visual changes, lightheadedness, poor intake) only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal Murphy's sign plus the volume-depletion and cardiac/pulmonary findings.
Build a differential led by Acute cholecystitis while explicitly excluding choledocholithiasis/cholangitis and ACS, and recognizing concurrent digoxin toxicity.
Order a focused workup (RUQ US, EKG, digoxin level, BMP/ketones, serial troponin) and interpret the integrated EKG and CXR.
Commit to Acute cholecystitis while treating digoxin toxicity and euglycemic ketoacidosis in parallel; manage with stabilization, renally dosed antibiotics, surgery, and admission to telemetry.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on a benign biliary cause and missing the digoxin toxicity and ACS questions
Giving IV calcium for the hyperkalemia in digoxin toxicity
Large fluid boluses into a congested heart
Discharging a surgical abdomen with concurrent toxic-metabolic emergencies
managementPearls: Renally dosed antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole)
Manage digoxin toxicity — hold digoxin, treat hyperkalemia (no IV calcium), DigiFab if unstable, atropine/pacing, repleted magnesium
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with a slow regularized ventricular response and scooped ST depression — digoxin toxicity in context; no acute STEMI.
Mild pulmonary vascular congestion/effusions of chronic heart failure — fluids must be cautious/titrated; no subdiaphragmatic free air.

Manage digoxin toxicity — hold digoxin, treat hyperkalemia (no IV calcium), DigiFab if unstable, atropine/pacing, repleted magnesium

imageInterpretationPearls: Read the patient's OWN tracing in clinical context — the same scooped ST depression is benign 'digoxin effect' or dangerous toxicity depending on level, renal function, potassium, and symptoms.
A chest film before resuscitation tells you how much fluid the heart will tolerate.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives (no STEMI, no free air), not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Takes a thorough medication history and asks permission before sensitive questions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
